aiMessages.content:
You are building a Telegram bot named Chain EX.

Purpose:
Chain EX helps users discover newly launched tokens using Dexscreener filters and social presence filtering.

The bot must behave exactly as described below.

🔹 GENERAL FLOW

When a user starts the bot:

Send a welcome message:
"Welcome to Chain EX 🔍
Find early tokens filtered by chain, age, market cap, and social presence."

Then begin a step-by-step guided filter selection process using Telegram inline buttons.

🔹 STEP 1: SELECT CHAIN

Prompt:
"Select Chain:"

Provide inline buttons:

ETH

SOL

BSC

BASE

Store the selected chain.

🔹 STEP 2: SELECT TOKEN AGE

Prompt:
"Select Token Age Range:"

Provide inline button options:

1 Day

2–3 Days

4–7 Days

1–2 Weeks

2–4 Weeks

1–3 Months

3–6 Months

6–12 Months

Store selected age range.

🔹 STEP 3: SELECT MARKET CAP

Prompt:
"Select Market Cap Range:"

Provide inline buttons:

10k–25k

25k–50k

50k–100k

100k–250k

250k–500k

Store selected market cap range.

🔹 STEP 4: TELEGRAM FILTER

Prompt:
"Require Telegram?"

Buttons:

Yes

No

If Yes → Only include tokens that have Telegram link available.
If No → Include tokens regardless.

Store selection.

🔹 STEP 5: DISCORD FILTER

Prompt:
"Require Discord?"

Buttons:

Yes

No

Store selection.

🔹 STEP 6: WEBSITE FILTER

Prompt:
"Require Website?"

Buttons:

Yes

No

Store selection.

🔹 DATA RETRIEVAL LOGIC

After all filters are selected:

Use Dexscreener API.

Filter by:

Selected chain

Token age range

Market cap range

For each token found:

Check if Telegram link exists (if required)

Check if Discord link exists (if required)

Check if Website link exists (if required)

Only include tokens that match ALL selected requirements.

Remove duplicates.

Only include tokens with active trading pairs.

🔹 RESULTS FORMAT

Display results in clean Telegram formatting.

Each result should show:

Token Name
Contract Address (CA)
Dexscreener Link
Telegram link (if available and/or required)
Discord link (if available and/or required)
Website link (if available and/or required)

Example format:

🔥 Token Name: ExampleToken
📜 CA: 0x1234...abcd
📊 Dexscreener: https://dexscreener.com/
...
💬 Telegram: https://t.me/
...
🌐 Website: https://...

Display 10 tokens per page.

Store results in session memory.

After displaying first 10:

Send message:
"Type /next to see more results."

🔹 /NEXT COMMAND

When user types /next:

Display the next 10 results from stored session.

If no more tokens available:
"No more tokens match your filters. Try adjusting your criteria."

🔹 ERROR HANDLING

If no tokens found:
"No tokens found with selected filters. Try widening age or market cap range."

If API fails:
"Unable to fetch data from Dexscreener. Please try again later."

🔹 UX REQUIREMENTS

Use inline keyboard buttons for all filter steps.

Store user selections in session state.

Clear session when new search starts.

Ensure results are clean and readable on Telegram mobile.

Avoid cluttered formatting.

🔹 OPTIONAL IMPROVEMENTS

Add /restart command to reset filters.

Add progress indicator like:
"Step 3 of 6"

Created a clean Chain EX Telegram bot project (grammY) with /start /help /restart /next, a 6-step inline-button wizard with “Step X of 6” prompts, in-memory per-user session state, Dexscreener retrieval + filtering (age, market cap, socials, active pairs), deterministic dedupe, and 10-per-page pagination. Added required exact user-facing error messages, production-safe logs, long polling via @grammyjs/runner, DOCS.md, README.md, and a minimal .env.sample with TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN only. Removed all AI and DB modules/envs to match the no-AI/no-other-integrations requirement.

Files touched (21): .env.sample, DOCS.md, README.md, package.json, project.json, src/bot.js…
Validation: ✅ project passes syntax/basic checks after this edit.

planMarkdown:
Bot: Chain EX (Telegram): Dexscreener-powered new token discovery bot

Summary:
Chain EX is a Telegram bot that guides users through a 6-step inline-button flow to discover newly launched tokens based on chain, token age, market cap range, and optional social presence requirements (Telegram/Discord/Website). After collecting filters, the bot queries Dexscreener, post-filters results to enforce the social link constraints, deduplicates, ensures only active pairs are included, and renders clean, paginated results (10 per page) with /next navigation.

This plan describes a production-ready, single-process Node.js (ESM) service built with grammY, using MongoDB for durable state where appropriate and in-memory session storage where persistence is optional. It focuses on reliable UX, robust API handling, and maintainable architecture without introducing additional infrastructure beyond the stated constraints.

Product goals:
Chain EX should make early token discovery simple and repeatable for Telegram users by:

1) Providing a guided, step-by-step inline button experience (no free-form typing for filters).
2) Enforcing strict filtering by chain, token age, market cap range, and optional social presence requirements.
3) Presenting results in a compact, mobile-friendly layout with clear links and contract address visibility.
4) Supporting pagination via /next, with results stored in per-user session state.
5) Handling empty results and upstream failures gracefully with user-friendly messages.

Success criteria:

1) A user can complete the 6 steps end-to-end without confusion, always seeing where they are in the process (optional step indicator).
2) Results are consistent for a given set of filters during a session, and /next continues from the correct offset.
3) Failures (Dexscreener downtime, timeouts, rate limits) do not crash the bot and yield a clear error message.
4) A new search clears prior selections and results reliably (via /restart or starting a new flow).

Non-goals (for initial release):

1) Portfolio tracking, watchlists, price alerts.
2) Advanced scoring or sentiment analysis.
3) Cross-user shared feeds or public leaderboards.

High-level architecture:
Single-process Node.js service (ESM) with grammY.

Core modules (separation of concerns):

1) Bot interface layer
   1) grammY bot instance, command handlers, callback query handlers
   2) Message rendering utilities (result formatting, prompts)
   3) Inline keyboard builders (all filter steps)

2) Conversation/state layer
   1) Per-user session state (current step, selected filters, results list, pagination cursor)
   2) Flow controller that transitions deterministically from Step 1 to Step 6
   3) Guards that prevent invalid transitions (e.g., pressing Step 4 buttons when still in Step 2)

3) Dexscreener integration layer
   1) HTTP client wrapper with timeouts, retries, and safe error mapping
   2) Query builder that maps user filters to available Dexscreener endpoints and parameters
   3) Normalization that converts API responses into a stable internal TokenResult model

4) Filtering and ranking layer
   1) Post-filtering for social presence requirements (Telegram/Discord/Website)
   2) Deduplication logic (by contract address per chain, and optionally by pair identifier)
   3) “Active trading pair” enforcement (based on API-provided flags/metrics; define a clear rule)
   4) Optional deterministic sorting (e.g., by newest pair first, then by volume or liquidity) to stabilize pagination

5) Persistence layer (MongoDB optional but recommended for production)
   1) Persist user preferences and last-used filters (optional feature)
   2) Persist minimal audit logs and error events (optional)
   3) Session storage can be in-memory initially; MongoDB-backed session storage can be added if restarts must not lose user pagination state

Runtime design notes:

1) All callback queries should be acknowledged quickly to keep Telegram UI responsive.
2) All Dexscreener fetches should be done after the final step, with a progress message (or editing the message) to avoid the appearance of a frozen bot.
3) Use message editing during the step flow to reduce chat clutter. For results pages, send new messages (or edit one “results” message) depending on desired UX; keep it consistent.
4) Ensure idempotency: re-clicking the same inline button should not corrupt session state; it should re-affirm selection and proceed consistently.

Deployment model:

1) A single Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram update delivery via webhook or long polling. Webhook is preferred for production reliability, but either works within constraints.
3) Environment variables for configuration, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN and MongoDB connection string.

Data model:
Session state (per user, stored in memory by default; optionally persisted in MongoDB):

1) userId: Telegram numeric user id
2) flowVersion: a number to invalidate old callback buttons after /restart
3) currentStep: integer 1..6 (plus states like “fetching” and “showingresults”)
4) filters:
   1) chain: one of ETH, SOL, BSC, BASE
   2) ageRange: one of the defined ranges
   3) marketCapRange: one of the defined ranges
   4) requireTelegram: boolean
   5) requireDiscord: boolean
   6) requireWebsite: boolean
5) results:
   1) items: array of normalized TokenResult
   2) cursor: integer offset (0, 10, 20, ...)
   3) pageSize: 10
   4) createdAt: timestamp to expire stale result sets
6) lastPromptMessageId: message id for editing prompts (optional but useful)

Normalized TokenResult (internal representation derived from Dexscreener):

1) chain: normalized chain identifier
2) tokenName: string
3) tokenSymbol: string (optional)
4) contractAddress: string
5) dexscreenerUrl: string
6) socials:
   1) telegramUrl: string or null
   2) discordUrl: string or null
   3) websiteUrl: string or null
7) pair:
   1) pairId: string
   2) pairUrl: string (optional if dexscreenerUrl already pair-specific)
   3) isActive: boolean (derived)
8) metrics (optional but recommended for debugging/quality):
   1) marketCap: number or null
   2) liquidity: number or null
   3) volume24h: number or null
   4) createdAt: timestamp or null

MongoDB collections (optional, recommended for production observability and UX improvements):

1) userprofiles
   1) userId
   2) createdAt, updatedAt
   3) lastUsedFilters (same shape as session filters)

2) botevents (optional)
   1) timestamp
   2) userId (if available)
   3) eventType (apierror, noresults, flowrestart, etc.)
   4) metadata (small JSON payload)

Data retention:

1) Session results should expire (e.g., 30–60 minutes) to avoid showing stale data and to limit memory usage.
2) userprofiles can be kept longer; allow deletion upon request if needed.

Deduplication key:

1) Primary: chain + contractAddress.
2) Secondary (if contract missing): chain + pairId.
3) Keep the “best” entry when duplicates exist, defined by a stable rule (e.g., most complete social links, highest liquidity, newest).

Key flows:
1) /start

1) Bot sends welcome message exactly:
   Welcome to Chain EX 🔍
   Find early tokens filtered by chain, age, market cap, and social presence.
2) Bot immediately begins Step 1 prompt with inline buttons.
3) Clear any existing session state for that user (filters, results, cursor) to ensure a fresh search.
4) Set flowVersion += 1 so old inline buttons become invalid.

2) Guided filter selection flow (inline buttons)

General rules:

1) Every step message includes an optional progress indicator, e.g., “Step 3 of 6” plus the prompt text.
2) On button click, validate that the callback matches the currentStep and flowVersion.
3) Store the selection in session.filters.
4) Advance currentStep and show the next prompt.
5) If a user clicks a button from an old message (old flowVersion), respond with a gentle instruction to /restart.

Step 1: Select Chain

1) Prompt: Select Chain:
2) Buttons: ETH, SOL, BSC, BASE
3) Store chain.

Step 2: Select Token Age Range

1) Prompt: Select Token Age Range:
2) Buttons: 1 Day; 2–3 Days; 4–7 Days; 1–2 Weeks; 2–4 Weeks; 1–3 Months; 3–6 Months; 6–12 Months
3) Store ageRange.

Step 3: Select Market Cap Range

1) Prompt: Select Market Cap Range:
2) Buttons: 10k–25k; 25k–50k; 50k–100k; 100k–250k; 250k–500k
3) Store marketCapRange.

Step 4: Telegram Filter

1) Prompt: Require Telegram?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireTelegram.

Step 5: Discord Filter

1) Prompt: Require Discord?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireDiscord.

Step 6: Website Filter

1) Prompt: Require Website?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireWebsite.

3) Data retrieval and filtering

After Step 6 selection:

1) Transition to a “fetching” state to prevent duplicate fetches.
2) Display a short progress message (or edit the step message) indicating the bot is fetching results.
3) Call Dexscreener API using the selected chain, age range, and market cap range.
4) Normalize results into TokenResult objects.
5) Enforce:
   1) Social presence requirements: if requireTelegram is true, telegramUrl must be present, similarly for Discord and Website.
   2) Remove duplicates.
   3) Only include tokens with active trading pairs. Define an explicit rule, such as: pairs with non-zero recent volume, or a boolean/field in response if available.
6) If after filtering there are zero results, return:
   No tokens found with selected filters. Try widening age or market cap range.
7) If API fails (timeout, 5xx, rate limited):
   Unable to fetch data from Dexscreener. Please try again later.
8) If results exist:
   1) Save results in session.results.items
   2) Set cursor = 0
   3) Render page 1 (items 0..9)
   4) Send message: Type /next to see more results.

4) Results formatting and pagination

Formatting goals:

1) Clean, readable on mobile.
2) Each token shown as a compact block with consistent ordering.
3) Avoid clutter: include only relevant links; if a social link is unavailable and not required, it can be omitted to keep messages short.

Per token block fields:

1) Token Name (and optionally symbol)
2) Contract Address (CA)
3) Dexscreener link
4) Telegram link if available and/or required
5) Discord link if available and/or required
6) Website link if available and/or required

Pagination rules:

1) Page size: 10.
2) /next increments cursor by 10.
3) If cursor >= results length, respond:
   No more tokens match your filters. Try adjusting your criteria.
4) If session has no stored results (user never ran a search or session expired), instruct user to /start or /restart.

5) /next

1) Validate that session.results exists and is not expired.
2) Render the next 10 results.
3) Update cursor.

6) /restart (optional improvement)

1) Clear session state (filters, results, cursor).
2) Increment flowVersion.
3) Re-run the start flow: welcome message can be omitted or re-sent; then show Step 1 prompt.

7) Safety and abuse prevention

1) Rate-limit expensive operations per user: avoid allowing repeated fetches in rapid succession (e.g., if user spam-clicks final step).
2) Add a global concurrency cap for Dexscreener requests to prevent overload in a single-process service.
3) Expire old callback buttons via flowVersion to reduce confusion.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:

1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API interactions.
3) Environment variable configuration, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.

Persistence:

1) MongoDB (already integrated) for optional durable storage (user profiles, last-used filters, minimal analytics, optional session persistence).
2) In-memory maps for session state and pagination if persistence is not required across restarts.

HTTP and API integration:

1) A standard Node.js HTTP client approach with explicit timeouts and bounded retries.
2) Dexscreener API integration through a dedicated module to isolate API changes and enforce normalization.

Hosting and deployment:

1) A generic Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram updates via webhook or long polling; webhook recommended for production.

Storage:

1) No special file storage required for this bot. If later needed (e.g., exporting reports), use simple filesystem storage or object storage.

Observability:

1) Structured logging to stdout.
2) Optional MongoDB-backed event collection for tracking error rates and empty-result frequency.

Constraints compliance:

1) No additional databases beyond MongoDB.
2) No Redis, queues, or other infra.
3) Single process design with careful in-process concurrency control.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:

1) Ensure the bot never crashes due to upstream API issues; handle all network and parsing errors.
2) Use timeouts for Dexscreener calls and cap retry attempts to avoid long hangs.
3) Gracefully degrade: if some token entries are missing fields, still render what is available.

Performance:

1) Keep Dexscreener response processing efficient (single pass normalization, set-based deduplication).
2) Store only what is needed for pagination to reduce memory.
3) Apply a reasonable maximum results limit per query to avoid memory blowups; if the API returns too many, take the top N according to a stable sort.

Security:

1) Do not log TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN or sensitive environment variables.
2) Validate and sanitize URLs before displaying (at minimum ensure they are strings and plausibly valid links).
3) Protect against callback spoofing by encoding flowVersion and step context in callback data.

UX quality:

1) Use inline keyboards for all filter steps.
2) Provide consistent step prompts and (optional) “Step X of 6” indicator.
3) Keep results formatting compact, avoid repeating unnecessary labels.
4) Edit previous prompt messages during the flow to reduce chat clutter.

Maintainability:

1) Keep filter definitions (chains, age ranges, market cap ranges) centralized in one configuration module.
2) Keep message text centralized to avoid accidental mismatches with required copy.
3) Isolate Dexscreener integration so API changes do not ripple across handlers.

Testing approach (practical):

1) Unit test normalization and post-filtering (social presence, dedup, active pair rule).
2) Integration test the flow controller transitions with simulated callback sequences.
3) Manual test on mobile to verify readability and button behavior.

Operational considerations:

1) Add basic health checks (internal) and startup validation (ensure TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN exists, MongoDB reachable if used).
2) Implement session expiry cleanup to avoid unbounded memory growth.
3) Log key events: search started, filters chosen, fetch succeeded/failed, zero results, pagination end.

Bot commands:
• /start — Start Chain EX and begin the guided filter selection flow. (usage: /start) [Core]
• /next — Show the next page of token results (10 per page) from your current session. (usage: /next) [Results]
• /restart — Reset filters and results, then start a new search from Step 1. (usage: /restart) [Core]

3) If cursor >= results length, respond:
   No more tokens match your filters. Try adjusting your criteria.
4) If session has no stored results (user never ran a search or session expired), instruct user to /start or /restart.

5) /next

1) Validate that session.results exists and is not expired.
2) Render the next 10 results.
3) Update cursor.

6) /restart (optional improvement)

1) Clear session state (filters, results, cursor).
2) Increment flowVersion.
3) Re-run the start flow: welcome message can be omitted or re-sent; then show Step 1 prompt.

7) Safety and abuse prevention

1) Rate-limit expensive operations per user: avoid allowing repeated fetches in rapid succession (e.g., if user spam-clicks final step).
2) Add a global concurrency cap for Dexscreener requests to prevent overload in a single-process service.
3) Expire old callback buttons via flowVersion to reduce confusion.

2) grammY for Telegram bot API interactions.

2) Dexscreener API integration through a dedicated module to isolate API changes and enforce normalization.

Hosting and deployment:

1) A generic Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram updates via webhook or long polling; webhook recommended for production.

Storage:

1) No special file storage required for this bot. If later needed (e.g., exporting reports), use simple filesystem storage or object storage.

Observability:

2) Optional MongoDB-backed event collection for tracking error rates and empty-result frequency.

Constraints compliance:

planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability:

1) Ensure the bot never crashes due to upstream API issues; handle all network and parsing errors.
2) Use timeouts for Dexscreener calls and cap retry attempts to avoid long hangs.
3) Gracefully degrade: if some token entries are missing fields, still render what is available.

Performance:

1) Keep Dexscreener response processing efficient (single pass normalization, set-based deduplication).
2) Store only what is needed for pagination to reduce memory.
3) Apply a reasonable maximum results limit per query to avoid memory blowups; if the API returns too many, take the top N according to a stable sort.

Security:

1) Do not log TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN or sensitive environment variables.
2) Validate and sanitize URLs before displaying (at minimum ensure they are strings and plausibly valid links).
3) Protect against callback spoofing by encoding flowVersion and step context in callback data.

UX quality:

1) Use inline keyboards for all filter steps.
2) Provide consistent step prompts and (optional) “Step X of 6” indicator.
3) Keep results formatting compact, avoid repeating unnecessary labels.
4) Edit previous prompt messages during the flow to reduce chat clutter.

Maintainability:

1) Keep filter definitions (chains, age ranges, market cap ranges) centralized in one configuration module.
2) Keep message text centralized to avoid accidental mismatches with required copy.
3) Isolate Dexscreener integration so API changes do not ripple across handlers.

Testing approach (practical):

1) Unit test normalization and post-filtering (social presence, dedup, active pair rule).
2) Integration test the flow controller transitions with simulated callback sequences.
3) Manual test on mobile to verify readability and button behavior.

Operational considerations:

1) Add basic health checks (internal) and startup validation (ensure TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN exists, MongoDB reachable if used).
2) Implement session expiry cleanup to avoid unbounded memory growth.
3) Log key events: search started, filters chosen, fetch succeeded/failed, zero results, pagination end.

DOCS.md.content:
Chain EX is a Telegram bot that helps you discover newly launched tokens by walking you through a guided filter wizard (inline buttons), then showing results in pages of 10.

Setup
1) Install dependencies
npm install

2) Configure environment
Copy .env.sample to .env and set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN.

3) Run locally
npm run dev

Commands and usage
/start
Starts a new search. It clears your previous filters and results, sends the welcome message, then begins Step 1 of 6.

/help
Shows a quick description of what the bot does and how to use /start or /restart, then /next to page results.

/restart
Clears your current search state and starts the wizard again from Step 1.

/next
Shows the next 10 results from your last completed search. If there are no more, the bot says:
No more tokens match your filters. Try adjusting your criteria.

Wizard flow
1) Select Chain
2) Select Token Age Range
3) Select Market Cap Range
4) Require Telegram?
5) Require Discord?
6) Require Website?

After Step 6, the bot fetches candidates from Dexscreener and filters them to match your selections.

Environment variables
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Your Telegram bot token from BotFather.

README.md.content:
Chain EX

What it does
A Telegram bot that guides users through an inline-button filter wizard to discover newly launched tokens using Dexscreener data, then paginates results with /next.

Features
1) Guided wizard (6 steps) using inline keyboards only
2) Filters by chain, token age, market cap, and required social links
3) Dedupes by contract address and keeps a deterministic best pair
4) Shows 10 results per page with /next

Architecture
1) src/index.js boots config and starts long polling via @grammyjs/runner
2) src/bot.js wires commands first, then callback handlers
3) src/services/dexscreener.js fetches and normalizes/filter results
4) src/lib/session.js stores per-user in-memory state

Setup
1) Install
npm install

2) Configure
Create a .env file with:
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=...

3) Run
npm run dev

Commands
/start
Starts a new wizard and clears previous state.

/help
Explains usage.

/restart
Clears state and starts wizard again.

/next
Shows next page of results (10 per page).

Dexscreener integration
The bot calls Dexscreener public API endpoints under https://api.dexscreener.com.
If the API is unavailable or returns unexpected data, the bot shows:
Unable to fetch data from Dexscreener. Please try again later.

Deployment
This is a single Node.js service. Set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN in your environment and run:
npm run build
npm start

Troubleshooting
1) If the bot does not start: confirm TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set.
2) If you see no results: widen age or market cap range, then /restart.
3) If callbacks stop working: /restart to refresh the wizard.

Extending
Add new commands in src/commands/*.js and register them in src/bot.js (before the callback handlers).
Add new filtering logic in src/services/dexscreener.js.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Chain EX (Telegram): Dexscreener-powered new token discovery bot

Summary:
Chain EX is a Telegram bot that guides users through a 6-step inline-button flow to discover newly launched tokens based on chain, token age, market cap range, and optional social presence requirements (Telegram/Discord/Website). After collecting filters, the bot queries Dexscreener, post-filters results to enforce the social link constraints, deduplicates, ensures only active pairs are included, and renders clean, paginated results (10 per page) with /next navigation.

This plan describes a production-ready, single-process Node.js (ESM) service built with grammY, using MongoDB for durable state where appropriate and in-memory session storage where persistence is optional. It focuses on reliable UX, robust API handling, and maintainable architecture without introducing additional infrastructure beyond the stated constraints.

Product goals:
Chain EX should make early token discovery simple and repeatable for Telegram users by:

1) Providing a guided, step-by-step inline button experience (no free-form typing for filters).
2) Enforcing strict filtering by chain, token age, market cap range, and optional social presence requirements.
3) Presenting results in a compact, mobile-friendly layout with clear links and contract address visibility.
4) Supporting pagination via /next, with results stored in per-user session state.
5) Handling empty results and upstream failures gracefully with user-friendly messages.

Success criteria:

1) A user can complete the 6 steps end-to-end without confusion, always seeing where they are in the process (optional step indicator).
2) Results are consistent for a given set of filters during a session, and /next continues from the correct offset.
3) Failures (Dexscreener downtime, timeouts, rate limits) do not crash the bot and yield a clear error message.
4) A new search clears prior selections and results reliably (via /restart or starting a new flow).

Non-goals (for initial release):

1) Portfolio tracking, watchlists, price alerts.
2) Advanced scoring or sentiment analysis.
3) Cross-user shared feeds or public leaderboards.

High-level architecture:
Single-process Node.js service (ESM) with grammY.

Core modules (separation of concerns):

1) Bot interface layer
   1) grammY bot instance, command handlers, callback query handlers
   2) Message rendering utilities (result formatting, prompts)
   3) Inline keyboard builders (all filter steps)

2) Conversation/state layer
   1) Per-user session state (current step, selected filters, results list, pagination cursor)
   2) Flow controller that transitions deterministically from Step 1 to Step 6
   3) Guards that prevent invalid transitions (e.g., pressing Step 4 buttons when still in Step 2)

3) Dexscreener integration layer
   1) HTTP client wrapper with timeouts, retries, and safe error mapping
   2) Query builder that maps user filters to available Dexscreener endpoints and parameters
   3) Normalization that converts API responses into a stable internal TokenResult model

4) Filtering and ranking layer
   1) Post-filtering for social presence requirements (Telegram/Discord/Website)
   2) Deduplication logic (by contract address per chain, and optionally by pair identifier)
   3) “Active trading pair” enforcement (based on API-provided flags/metrics; define a clear rule)
   4) Optional deterministic sorting (e.g., by newest pair first, then by volume or liquidity) to stabilize pagination

5) Persistence layer (MongoDB optional but recommended for production)
   1) Persist user preferences and last-used filters (optional feature)
   2) Persist minimal audit logs and error events (optional)
   3) Session storage can be in-memory initially; MongoDB-backed session storage can be added if restarts must not lose user pagination state

Runtime design notes:

1) All callback queries should be acknowledged quickly to keep Telegram UI responsive.
2) All Dexscreener fetches should be done after the final step, with a progress message (or editing the message) to avoid the appearance of a frozen bot.
3) Use message editing during the step flow to reduce chat clutter. For results pages, send new messages (or edit one “results” message) depending on desired UX; keep it consistent.
4) Ensure idempotency: re-clicking the same inline button should not corrupt session state; it should re-affirm selection and proceed consistently.

Deployment model:

1) A single Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram update delivery via webhook or long polling. Webhook is preferred for production reliability, but either works within constraints.
3) Environment variables for configuration, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN and MongoDB connection string.

Data model:
Session state (per user, stored in memory by default; optionally persisted in MongoDB):

1) userId: Telegram numeric user id
2) flowVersion: a number to invalidate old callback buttons after /restart
3) currentStep: integer 1..6 (plus states like “fetching” and “showingresults”)
4) filters:
   1) chain: one of ETH, SOL, BSC, BASE
   2) ageRange: one of the defined ranges
   3) marketCapRange: one of the defined ranges
   4) requireTelegram: boolean
   5) requireDiscord: boolean
   6) requireWebsite: boolean
5) results:
   1) items: array of normalized TokenResult
   2) cursor: integer offset (0, 10, 20, ...)
   3) pageSize: 10
   4) createdAt: timestamp to expire stale result sets
6) lastPromptMessageId: message id for editing prompts (optional but useful)

Normalized TokenResult (internal representation derived from Dexscreener):

1) chain: normalized chain identifier
2) tokenName: string
3) tokenSymbol: string (optional)
4) contractAddress: string
5) dexscreenerUrl: string
6) socials:
   1) telegramUrl: string or null
   2) discordUrl: string or null
   3) websiteUrl: string or null
7) pair:
   1) pairId: string
   2) pairUrl: string (optional if dexscreenerUrl already pair-specific)
   3) isActive: boolean (derived)
8) metrics (optional but recommended for debugging/quality):
   1) marketCap: number or null
   2) liquidity: number or null
   3) volume24h: number or null
   4) createdAt: timestamp or null

MongoDB collections (optional, recommended for production observability and UX improvements):

1) userprofiles
   1) userId
   2) createdAt, updatedAt
   3) lastUsedFilters (same shape as session filters)

2) botevents (optional)
   1) timestamp
   2) userId (if available)
   3) eventType (apierror, noresults, flowrestart, etc.)
   4) metadata (small JSON payload)

Data retention:

1) Session results should expire (e.g., 30–60 minutes) to avoid showing stale data and to limit memory usage.
2) userprofiles can be kept longer; allow deletion upon request if needed.

Deduplication key:

1) Primary: chain + contractAddress.
2) Secondary (if contract missing): chain + pairId.
3) Keep the “best” entry when duplicates exist, defined by a stable rule (e.g., most complete social links, highest liquidity, newest).

Key flows:
1) /start

1) Bot sends welcome message exactly:
   Welcome to Chain EX 🔍
   Find early tokens filtered by chain, age, market cap, and social presence.
2) Bot immediately begins Step 1 prompt with inline buttons.
3) Clear any existing session state for that user (filters, results, cursor) to ensure a fresh search.
4) Set flowVersion += 1 so old inline buttons become invalid.

2) Guided filter selection flow (inline buttons)

General rules:

1) Every step message includes an optional progress indicator, e.g., “Step 3 of 6” plus the prompt text.
2) On button click, validate that the callback matches the currentStep and flowVersion.
3) Store the selection in session.filters.
4) Advance currentStep and show the next prompt.
5) If a user clicks a button from an old message (old flowVersion), respond with a gentle instruction to /restart.

Step 1: Select Chain

1) Prompt: Select Chain:
2) Buttons: ETH, SOL, BSC, BASE
3) Store chain.

Step 2: Select Token Age Range

1) Prompt: Select Token Age Range:
2) Buttons: 1 Day; 2–3 Days; 4–7 Days; 1–2 Weeks; 2–4 Weeks; 1–3 Months; 3–6 Months; 6–12 Months
3) Store ageRange.

Step 3: Select Market Cap Range

1) Prompt: Select Market Cap Range:
2) Buttons: 10k–25k; 25k–50k; 50k–100k; 100k–250k; 250k–500k
3) Store marketCapRange.

Step 4: Telegram Filter

1) Prompt: Require Telegram?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireTelegram.

Step 5: Discord Filter

1) Prompt: Require Discord?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireDiscord.

Step 6: Website Filter

1) Prompt: Require Website?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireWebsite.

3) Data retrieval and filtering

After Step 6 selection:

1) Transition to a “fetching” state to prevent duplicate fetches.
2) Display a short progress message (or edit the step message) indicating the bot is fetching results.
3) Call Dexscreener API using the selected chain, age range, and market cap range.
4) Normalize results into TokenResult objects.
5) Enforce:
   1) Social presence requirements: if requireTelegram is true, telegramUrl must be present, similarly for Discord and Website.
   2) Remove duplicates.
   3) Only include tokens with active trading pairs. Define an explicit rule, such as: pairs with non-zero recent volume, or a boolean/field in response if available.
6) If after filtering there are zero results, return:
   No tokens found with selected filters. Try widening age or market cap range.
7) If API fails (timeout, 5xx, rate limited):
   Unable to fetch data from Dexscreener. Please try again later.
8) If results exist:
   1) Save results in session.results.items
   2) Set cursor = 0
   3) Render page 1 (items 0..9)
   4) Send message: Type /next to see more results.

4) Results formatting and pagination

Formatting goals:

1) Clean, readable on mobile.
2) Each token shown as a compact block with consistent ordering.
3) Avoid clutter: include only relevant links; if a social link is unavailable and not required, it can be omitted to keep messages short.

Per token block fields:

1) Token Name (and optionally symbol)
2) Contract Address (CA)
3) Dexscreener link
4) Telegram link if available and/or required
5) Discord link if available and/or required
6) Website link if available and/or required

Pagination rules:

1) Page size: 10.
2) /next increments cursor by 10.
3) If cursor >= results length, respond:
   No more tokens match your filters. Try adjusting your criteria.
4) If session has no stored results (user never ran a search or session expired), instruct user to /start or /restart.

5) /next

1) Validate that session.results exists and is not expired.
2) Render the next 10 results.
3) Update cursor.

6) /restart (optional improvement)

1) Clear session state (filters, results, cursor).
2) Increment flowVersion.
3) Re-run the start flow: welcome message can be omitted or re-sent; then show Step 1 prompt.

7) Safety and abuse prevention

1) Rate-limit expensive operations per user: avoid allowing repeated fetches in rapid succession (e.g., if user spam-clicks final step).
2) Add a global concurrency cap for Dexscreener requests to prevent overload in a single-process service.
3) Expire old callback buttons via flowVersion to reduce confusion.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:

1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API interactions.
3) Environment variable configuration, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.

Persistence:

1) MongoDB (already integrated) for optional durable storage (user profiles, last-used filters, minimal analytics, optional session persistence).
2) In-memory maps for session state and pagination if persistence is not required across restarts.

HTTP and API integration:

1) A standard Node.js HTTP client approach with explicit timeouts and bounded retries.
2) Dexscreener API integration through a dedicated module to isolate API changes and enforce normalization.

Hosting and deployment:

1) A generic Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram updates via webhook or long polling; webhook recommended for production.

Storage:

1) No special file storage required for this bot. If later needed (e.g., exporting reports), use simple filesystem storage or object storage.

Observability:

1) Structured logging to stdout.
2) Optional MongoDB-backed event collection for tracking error rates and empty-result frequency.

Constraints compliance:

1) No additional databases beyond MongoDB.
2) No Redis, queues, or other infra.
3) Single process design with careful in-process concurrency control.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:

1) Ensure the bot never crashes due to upstream API issues; handle all network and parsing errors.
2) Use timeouts for Dexscreener calls and cap retry attempts to avoid long hangs.
3) Gracefully degrade: if some token entries are missing fields, still render what is available.

Performance:

1) Keep Dexscreener response processing efficient (single pass normalization, set-based deduplication).
2) Store only what is needed for pagination to reduce memory.
3) Apply a reasonable maximum results limit per query to avoid memory blowups; if the API returns too many, take the top N according to a stable sort.

Security:

1) Do not log TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN or sensitive environment variables.
2) Validate and sanitize URLs before displaying (at minimum ensure they are strings and plausibly valid links).
3) Protect against callback spoofing by encoding flowVersion and step context in callback data.

UX quality:

1) Use inline keyboards for all filter steps.
2) Provide consistent step prompts and (optional) “Step X of 6” indicator.
3) Keep results formatting compact, avoid repeating unnecessary labels.
4) Edit previous prompt messages during the flow to reduce chat clutter.

Maintainability:

1) Keep filter definitions (chains, age ranges, market cap ranges) centralized in one configuration module.
2) Keep message text centralized to avoid accidental mismatches with required copy.
3) Isolate Dexscreener integration so API changes do not ripple across handlers.

Testing approach (practical):

1) Unit test normalization and post-filtering (social presence, dedup, active pair rule).
2) Integration test the flow controller transitions with simulated callback sequences.
3) Manual test on mobile to verify readability and button behavior.

Operational considerations:

1) Add basic health checks (internal) and startup validation (ensure TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN exists, MongoDB reachable if used).
2) Implement session expiry cleanup to avoid unbounded memory growth.
3) Log key events: search started, filters chosen, fetch succeeded/failed, zero results, pagination end.

Bot commands:
• /start — Start Chain EX and begin the guided filter selection flow. (usage: /start) [Core]
• /next — Show the next page of token results (10 per page) from your current session. (usage: /next) [Results]
• /restart — Reset filters and results, then start a new search from Step 1. (usage: /restart) [Core]

planSections.productGoals:
Chain EX should make early token discovery simple and repeatable for Telegram users by:

1) Providing a guided, step-by-step inline button experience (no free-form typing for filters).
2) Enforcing strict filtering by chain, token age, market cap range, and optional social presence requirements.
3) Presenting results in a compact, mobile-friendly layout with clear links and contract address visibility.
4) Supporting pagination via /next, with results stored in per-user session state.
5) Handling empty results and upstream failures gracefully with user-friendly messages.

Success criteria:

1) A user can complete the 6 steps end-to-end without confusion, always seeing where they are in the process (optional step indicator).
2) Results are consistent for a given set of filters during a session, and /next continues from the correct offset.
3) Failures (Dexscreener downtime, timeouts, rate limits) do not crash the bot and yield a clear error message.
4) A new search clears prior selections and results reliably (via /restart or starting a new flow).

Non-goals (for initial release):

1) Portfolio tracking, watchlists, price alerts.
2) Advanced scoring or sentiment analysis.
3) Cross-user shared feeds or public leaderboards.
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
Single-process Node.js service (ESM) with grammY.

Core modules (separation of concerns):

1) Bot interface layer
   1) grammY bot instance, command handlers, callback query handlers
   2) Message rendering utilities (result formatting, prompts)
   3) Inline keyboard builders (all filter steps)

2) Conversation/state layer
   1) Per-user session state (current step, selected filters, results list, pagination cursor)
   2) Flow controller that transitions deterministically from Step 1 to Step 6
   3) Guards that prevent invalid transitions (e.g., pressing Step 4 buttons when still in Step 2)

3) Dexscreener integration layer
   1) HTTP client wrapper with timeouts, retries, and safe error mapping
   2) Query builder that maps user filters to available Dexscreener endpoints and parameters
   3) Normalization that converts API responses into a stable internal TokenResult model

4) Filtering and ranking layer
   1) Post-filtering for social presence requirements (Telegram/Discord/Website)
   2) Deduplication logic (by contract address per chain, and optionally by pair identifier)
   3) “Active trading pair” enforcement (based on API-provided flags/metrics; define a clear rule)
   4) Optional deterministic sorting (e.g., by newest pair first, then by volume or liquidity) to stabilize pagination

5) Persistence layer (MongoDB optional but recommended for production)
   1) Persist user preferences and last-used filters (optional feature)
   2) Persist minimal audit logs and error events (optional)
   3) Session storage can be in-memory initially; MongoDB-backed session storage can be added if restarts must not lose user pagination state

Runtime design notes:

1) All callback queries should be acknowledged quickly to keep Telegram UI responsive.
2) All Dexscreener fetches should be done after the final step, with a progress message (or editing the message) to avoid the appearance of a frozen bot.
3) Use message editing during the step flow to reduce chat clutter. For results pages, send new messages (or edit one “results” message) depending on desired UX; keep it consistent.
4) Ensure idempotency: re-clicking the same inline button should not corrupt session state; it should re-affirm selection and proceed consistently.

Deployment model:

1) A single Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram update delivery via webhook or long polling. Webhook is preferred for production reliability, but either works within constraints.
3) Environment variables for configuration, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN and MongoDB connection string.
planSections.dataModel:
Session state (per user, stored in memory by default; optionally persisted in MongoDB):

1) userId: Telegram numeric user id
2) flowVersion: a number to invalidate old callback buttons after /restart
3) currentStep: integer 1..6 (plus states like “fetching” and “showingresults”)
4) filters:
   1) chain: one of ETH, SOL, BSC, BASE
   2) ageRange: one of the defined ranges
   3) marketCapRange: one of the defined ranges
   4) requireTelegram: boolean
   5) requireDiscord: boolean
   6) requireWebsite: boolean
5) results:
   1) items: array of normalized TokenResult
   2) cursor: integer offset (0, 10, 20, ...)
   3) pageSize: 10
   4) createdAt: timestamp to expire stale result sets
6) lastPromptMessageId: message id for editing prompts (optional but useful)

Normalized TokenResult (internal representation derived from Dexscreener):

1) chain: normalized chain identifier
2) tokenName: string
3) tokenSymbol: string (optional)
4) contractAddress: string
5) dexscreenerUrl: string
6) socials:
   1) telegramUrl: string or null
   2) discordUrl: string or null
   3) websiteUrl: string or null
7) pair:
   1) pairId: string
   2) pairUrl: string (optional if dexscreenerUrl already pair-specific)
   3) isActive: boolean (derived)
8) metrics (optional but recommended for debugging/quality):
   1) marketCap: number or null
   2) liquidity: number or null
   3) volume24h: number or null
   4) createdAt: timestamp or null

MongoDB collections (optional, recommended for production observability and UX improvements):

1) userprofiles
   1) userId
   2) createdAt, updatedAt
   3) lastUsedFilters (same shape as session filters)

2) botevents (optional)
   1) timestamp
   2) userId (if available)
   3) eventType (apierror, noresults, flowrestart, etc.)
   4) metadata (small JSON payload)

Data retention:

1) Session results should expire (e.g., 30–60 minutes) to avoid showing stale data and to limit memory usage.
2) userprofiles can be kept longer; allow deletion upon request if needed.

Deduplication key:

1) Primary: chain + contractAddress.
2) Secondary (if contract missing): chain + pairId.
3) Keep the “best” entry when duplicates exist, defined by a stable rule (e.g., most complete social links, highest liquidity, newest).
planSections.keyFlows:
1) /start

1) Bot sends welcome message exactly:
   Welcome to Chain EX 🔍
   Find early tokens filtered by chain, age, market cap, and social presence.
2) Bot immediately begins Step 1 prompt with inline buttons.
3) Clear any existing session state for that user (filters, results, cursor) to ensure a fresh search.
4) Set flowVersion += 1 so old inline buttons become invalid.

2) Guided filter selection flow (inline buttons)

General rules:

1) Every step message includes an optional progress indicator, e.g., “Step 3 of 6” plus the prompt text.
2) On button click, validate that the callback matches the currentStep and flowVersion.
3) Store the selection in session.filters.
4) Advance currentStep and show the next prompt.
5) If a user clicks a button from an old message (old flowVersion), respond with a gentle instruction to /restart.

Step 1: Select Chain

1) Prompt: Select Chain:
2) Buttons: ETH, SOL, BSC, BASE
3) Store chain.

Step 2: Select Token Age Range

1) Prompt: Select Token Age Range:
2) Buttons: 1 Day; 2–3 Days; 4–7 Days; 1–2 Weeks; 2–4 Weeks; 1–3 Months; 3–6 Months; 6–12 Months
3) Store ageRange.

Step 3: Select Market Cap Range

1) Prompt: Select Market Cap Range:
2) Buttons: 10k–25k; 25k–50k; 50k–100k; 100k–250k; 250k–500k
3) Store marketCapRange.

Step 4: Telegram Filter

1) Prompt: Require Telegram?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireTelegram.

Step 5: Discord Filter

1) Prompt: Require Discord?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireDiscord.

Step 6: Website Filter

1) Prompt: Require Website?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireWebsite.

3) Data retrieval and filtering

After Step 6 selection:

1) Transition to a “fetching” state to prevent duplicate fetches.
2) Display a short progress message (or edit the step message) indicating the bot is fetching results.
3) Call Dexscreener API using the selected chain, age range, and market cap range.
4) Normalize results into TokenResult objects.
5) Enforce:
   1) Social presence requirements: if requireTelegram is true, telegramUrl must be present, similarly for Discord and Website.
   2) Remove duplicates.
   3) Only include tokens with active trading pairs. Define an explicit rule, such as: pairs with non-zero recent volume, or a boolean/field in response if available.
6) If after filtering there are zero results, return:
   No tokens found with selected filters. Try widening age or market cap range.
7) If API fails (timeout, 5xx, rate limited):
   Unable to fetch data from Dexscreener. Please try again later.
8) If results exist:
   1) Save results in session.results.items
   2) Set cursor = 0
   3) Render page 1 (items 0..9)
   4) Send message: Type /next to see more results.

4) Results formatting and pagination

Formatting goals:

1) Clean, readable on mobile.
2) Each token shown as a compact block with consistent ordering.
3) Avoid clutter: include only relevant links; if a social link is unavailable and not required, it can be omitted to keep messages short.

Per token block fields:

1) Token Name (and optionally symbol)
2) Contract Address (CA)
3) Dexscreener link
4) Telegram link if available and/or required
5) Discord link if available and/or required
6) Website link if available and/or required

Pagination rules:

1) Page size: 10.
2) /next increments cursor by 10.
3) If cursor >= results length, respond:
   No more tokens match your filters. Try adjusting your criteria.
4) If session has no stored results (user never ran a search or session expired), instruct user to /start or /restart.

5) /next

1) Validate that session.results exists and is not expired.
2) Render the next 10 results.
3) Update cursor.

6) /restart (optional improvement)

1) Clear session state (filters, results, cursor).
2) Increment flowVersion.
3) Re-run the start flow: welcome message can be omitted or re-sent; then show Step 1 prompt.

7) Safety and abuse prevention

1) Rate-limit expensive operations per user: avoid allowing repeated fetches in rapid succession (e.g., if user spam-clicks final step).
2) Add a global concurrency cap for Dexscreener requests to prevent overload in a single-process service.
3) Expire old callback buttons via flowVersion to reduce confusion.

DOCS.md.content:
Chain EX is a Telegram bot that helps you discover newly launched tokens by walking you through a guided filter wizard (inline buttons), then showing results in pages of 10.

Setup
1) Install dependencies
npm install

2) Configure environment
Copy .env.sample to .env and set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN.

3) Run locally
npm run dev

Commands and usage
/start
Starts a new search. It clears your previous filters and results, sends the welcome message, then begins Step 1 of 6.

/help
Shows a quick description of what the bot does and how to use /start or /restart, then /next to page results.

/restart
Clears your current search state and starts the wizard again from Step 1.

/next
Shows the next 10 results from your last completed search. If there are no more, the bot says:
No more tokens match your filters. Try adjusting your criteria.

Wizard flow
1) Select Chain
2) Select Token Age Range
3) Select Market Cap Range
4) Require Telegram?
5) Require Discord?
6) Require Website?

After Step 6, the bot fetches candidates from Dexscreener and filters them to match your selections.

Environment variables
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Your Telegram bot token from BotFather.

README.md.content:
Chain EX

What it does
A Telegram bot that guides users through an inline-button filter wizard to discover newly launched tokens using Dexscreener data, then paginates results with /next.

Features
1) Guided wizard (6 steps) using inline keyboards only
2) Filters by chain, token age, market cap, and required social links
3) Dedupes by contract address and keeps a deterministic best pair
4) Shows 10 results per page with /next

Architecture
1) src/index.js boots config and starts long polling via @grammyjs/runner
2) src/bot.js wires commands first, then callback handlers
3) src/services/dexscreener.js fetches and normalizes/filter results
4) src/lib/session.js stores per-user in-memory state

Setup
1) Install
npm install

2) Configure
Create a .env file with:
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=...

3) Run
npm run dev

Commands
/start
Starts a new wizard and clears previous state.

/help
Explains usage.

/restart
Clears state and starts wizard again.

/next
Shows next page of results (10 per page).

Dexscreener integration
The bot calls Dexscreener public API endpoints under https://api.dexscreener.com.
If the API is unavailable or returns unexpected data, the bot shows:
Unable to fetch data from Dexscreener. Please try again later.

Deployment
This is a single Node.js service. Set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN in your environment and run:
npm run build
npm start

Troubleshooting
1) If the bot does not start: confirm TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set.
2) If you see no results: widen age or market cap range, then /restart.
3) If callbacks stop working: /restart to refresh the wizard.

Extending
Add new commands in src/commands/*.js and register them in src/bot.js (before the callback handlers).
Add new filtering logic in src/services/dexscreener.js.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Chain EX (Telegram): Dexscreener-powered new token discovery bot

Summary:
Chain EX is a Telegram bot that guides users through a 6-step inline-button flow to discover newly launched tokens based on chain, token age, market cap range, and optional social presence requirements (Telegram/Discord/Website). After collecting filters, the bot queries Dexscreener, post-filters results to enforce the social link constraints, deduplicates, ensures only active pairs are included, and renders clean, paginated results (10 per page) with /next navigation.

This plan describes a production-ready, single-process Node.js (ESM) service built with grammY, using MongoDB for durable state where appropriate and in-memory session storage where persistence is optional. It focuses on reliable UX, robust API handling, and maintainable architecture without introducing additional infrastructure beyond the stated constraints.

Product goals:
Chain EX should make early token discovery simple and repeatable for Telegram users by:

1) Providing a guided, step-by-step inline button experience (no free-form typing for filters).
2) Enforcing strict filtering by chain, token age, market cap range, and optional social presence requirements.
3) Presenting results in a compact, mobile-friendly layout with clear links and contract address visibility.
4) Supporting pagination via /next, with results stored in per-user session state.
5) Handling empty results and upstream failures gracefully with user-friendly messages.

Success criteria:

1) A user can complete the 6 steps end-to-end without confusion, always seeing where they are in the process (optional step indicator).
2) Results are consistent for a given set of filters during a session, and /next continues from the correct offset.
3) Failures (Dexscreener downtime, timeouts, rate limits) do not crash the bot and yield a clear error message.
4) A new search clears prior selections and results reliably (via /restart or starting a new flow).

Non-goals (for initial release):

1) Portfolio tracking, watchlists, price alerts.
2) Advanced scoring or sentiment analysis.
3) Cross-user shared feeds or public leaderboards.

High-level architecture:
Single-process Node.js service (ESM) with grammY.

Core modules (separation of concerns):

1) Bot interface layer
   1) grammY bot instance, command handlers, callback query handlers
   2) Message rendering utilities (result formatting, prompts)
   3) Inline keyboard builders (all filter steps)

2) Conversation/state layer
   1) Per-user session state (current step, selected filters, results list, pagination cursor)
   2) Flow controller that transitions deterministically from Step 1 to Step 6
   3) Guards that prevent invalid transitions (e.g., pressing Step 4 buttons when still in Step 2)

3) Dexscreener integration layer
   1) HTTP client wrapper with timeouts, retries, and safe error mapping
   2) Query builder that maps user filters to available Dexscreener endpoints and parameters
   3) Normalization that converts API responses into a stable internal TokenResult model

4) Filtering and ranking layer
   1) Post-filtering for social presence requirements (Telegram/Discord/Website)
   2) Deduplication logic (by contract address per chain, and optionally by pair identifier)
   3) “Active trading pair” enforcement (based on API-provided flags/metrics; define a clear rule)
   4) Optional deterministic sorting (e.g., by newest pair first, then by volume or liquidity) to stabilize pagination

5) Persistence layer (MongoDB optional but recommended for production)
   1) Persist user preferences and last-used filters (optional feature)
   2) Persist minimal audit logs and error events (optional)
   3) Session storage can be in-memory initially; MongoDB-backed session storage can be added if restarts must not lose user pagination state

Runtime design notes:

1) All callback queries should be acknowledged quickly to keep Telegram UI responsive.
2) All Dexscreener fetches should be done after the final step, with a progress message (or editing the message) to avoid the appearance of a frozen bot.
3) Use message editing during the step flow to reduce chat clutter. For results pages, send new messages (or edit one “results” message) depending on desired UX; keep it consistent.
4) Ensure idempotency: re-clicking the same inline button should not corrupt session state; it should re-affirm selection and proceed consistently.

Deployment model:

1) A single Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram update delivery via webhook or long polling. Webhook is preferred for production reliability, but either works within constraints.
3) Environment variables for configuration, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN and MongoDB connection string.

Data model:
Session state (per user, stored in memory by default; optionally persisted in MongoDB):

1) userId: Telegram numeric user id
2) flowVersion: a number to invalidate old callback buttons after /restart
3) currentStep: integer 1..6 (plus states like “fetching” and “showingresults”)
4) filters:
   1) chain: one of ETH, SOL, BSC, BASE
   2) ageRange: one of the defined ranges
   3) marketCapRange: one of the defined ranges
   4) requireTelegram: boolean
   5) requireDiscord: boolean
   6) requireWebsite: boolean
5) results:
   1) items: array of normalized TokenResult
   2) cursor: integer offset (0, 10, 20, ...)
   3) pageSize: 10
   4) createdAt: timestamp to expire stale result sets
6) lastPromptMessageId: message id for editing prompts (optional but useful)

Normalized TokenResult (internal representation derived from Dexscreener):

1) chain: normalized chain identifier
2) tokenName: string
3) tokenSymbol: string (optional)
4) contractAddress: string
5) dexscreenerUrl: string
6) socials:
   1) telegramUrl: string or null
   2) discordUrl: string or null
   3) websiteUrl: string or null
7) pair:
   1) pairId: string
   2) pairUrl: string (optional if dexscreenerUrl already pair-specific)
   3) isActive: boolean (derived)
8) metrics (optional but recommended for debugging/quality):
   1) marketCap: number or null
   2) liquidity: number or null
   3) volume24h: number or null
   4) createdAt: timestamp or null

MongoDB collections (optional, recommended for production observability and UX improvements):

1) userprofiles
   1) userId
   2) createdAt, updatedAt
   3) lastUsedFilters (same shape as session filters)

2) botevents (optional)
   1) timestamp
   2) userId (if available)
   3) eventType (apierror, noresults, flowrestart, etc.)
   4) metadata (small JSON payload)

Data retention:

1) Session results should expire (e.g., 30–60 minutes) to avoid showing stale data and to limit memory usage.
2) userprofiles can be kept longer; allow deletion upon request if needed.

Deduplication key:

1) Primary: chain + contractAddress.
2) Secondary (if contract missing): chain + pairId.
3) Keep the “best” entry when duplicates exist, defined by a stable rule (e.g., most complete social links, highest liquidity, newest).

Key flows:
1) /start

1) Bot sends welcome message exactly:
   Welcome to Chain EX 🔍
   Find early tokens filtered by chain, age, market cap, and social presence.
2) Bot immediately begins Step 1 prompt with inline buttons.
3) Clear any existing session state for that user (filters, results, cursor) to ensure a fresh search.
4) Set flowVersion += 1 so old inline buttons become invalid.

2) Guided filter selection flow (inline buttons)

General rules:

1) Every step message includes an optional progress indicator, e.g., “Step 3 of 6” plus the prompt text.
2) On button click, validate that the callback matches the currentStep and flowVersion.
3) Store the selection in session.filters.
4) Advance currentStep and show the next prompt.
5) If a user clicks a button from an old message (old flowVersion), respond with a gentle instruction to /restart.

Step 1: Select Chain

1) Prompt: Select Chain:
2) Buttons: ETH, SOL, BSC, BASE
3) Store chain.

Step 2: Select Token Age Range

1) Prompt: Select Token Age Range:
2) Buttons: 1 Day; 2–3 Days; 4–7 Days; 1–2 Weeks; 2–4 Weeks; 1–3 Months; 3–6 Months; 6–12 Months
3) Store ageRange.

Step 3: Select Market Cap Range

1) Prompt: Select Market Cap Range:
2) Buttons: 10k–25k; 25k–50k; 50k–100k; 100k–250k; 250k–500k
3) Store marketCapRange.

Step 4: Telegram Filter

1) Prompt: Require Telegram?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireTelegram.

Step 5: Discord Filter

1) Prompt: Require Discord?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireDiscord.

Step 6: Website Filter

1) Prompt: Require Website?
2) Buttons: Yes / No
3) Store requireWebsite.

3) Data retrieval and filtering

After Step 6 selection:

1) Transition to a “fetching” state to prevent duplicate fetches.
2) Display a short progress message (or edit the step message) indicating the bot is fetching results.
3) Call Dexscreener API using the selected chain, age range, and market cap range.
4) Normalize results into TokenResult objects.
5) Enforce:
   1) Social presence requirements: if requireTelegram is true, telegramUrl must be present, similarly for Discord and Website.
   2) Remove duplicates.
   3) Only include tokens with active trading pairs. Define an explicit rule, such as: pairs with non-zero recent volume, or a boolean/field in response if available.
6) If after filtering there are zero results, return:
   No tokens found with selected filters. Try widening age or market cap range.
7) If API fails (timeout, 5xx, rate limited):
   Unable to fetch data from Dexscreener. Please try again later.
8) If results exist:
   1) Save results in session.results.items
   2) Set cursor = 0
   3) Render page 1 (items 0..9)
   4) Send message: Type /next to see more results.

4) Results formatting and pagination

Formatting goals:

1) Clean, readable on mobile.
2) Each token shown as a compact block with consistent ordering.
3) Avoid clutter: include only relevant links; if a social link is unavailable and not required, it can be omitted to keep messages short.

Per token block fields:

1) Token Name (and optionally symbol)
2) Contract Address (CA)
3) Dexscreener link
4) Telegram link if available and/or required
5) Discord link if available and/or required
6) Website link if available and/or required

Pagination rules:

1) Page size: 10.
2) /next increments cursor by 10.
3) If cursor >= results length, respond:
   No more tokens match your filters. Try adjusting your criteria.
4) If session has no stored results (user never ran a search or session expired), instruct user to /start or /restart.

5) /next

1) Validate that session.results exists and is not expired.
2) Render the next 10 results.
3) Update cursor.

6) /restart (optional improvement)

1) Clear session state (filters, results, cursor).
2) Increment flowVersion.
3) Re-run the start flow: welcome message can be omitted or re-sent; then show Step 1 prompt.

7) Safety and abuse prevention

1) Rate-limit expensive operations per user: avoid allowing repeated fetches in rapid succession (e.g., if user spam-clicks final step).
2) Add a global concurrency cap for Dexscreener requests to prevent overload in a single-process service.
3) Expire old callback buttons via flowVersion to reduce confusion.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:

1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API interactions.
3) Environment variable configuration, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.

Persistence:

1) MongoDB (already integrated) for optional durable storage (user profiles, last-used filters, minimal analytics, optional session persistence).
2) In-memory maps for session state and pagination if persistence is not required across restarts.

HTTP and API integration:

1) A standard Node.js HTTP client approach with explicit timeouts and bounded retries.
2) Dexscreener API integration through a dedicated module to isolate API changes and enforce normalization.

Hosting and deployment:

1) A generic Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Telegram updates via webhook or long polling; webhook recommended for production.

Storage:

1) No special file storage required for this bot. If later needed (e.g., exporting reports), use simple filesystem storage or object storage.

Observability:

1) Structured logging to stdout.
2) Optional MongoDB-backed event collection for tracking error rates and empty-result frequency.

Constraints compliance:

1) No additional databases beyond MongoDB.
2) No Redis, queues, or other infra.
3) Single process design with careful in-process concurrency control.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:

1) Ensure the bot never crashes due to upstream API issues; handle all network and parsing errors.
2) Use timeouts for Dexscreener calls and cap retry attempts to avoid long hangs.
3) Gracefully degrade: if some token entries are missing fields, still render what is available.

Performance:

1) Keep Dexscreener response processing efficient (single pass normalization, set-based deduplication).
2) Store only what is needed for pagination to reduce memory.
3) Apply a reasonable maximum results limit per query to avoid memory blowups; if the API returns too many, take the top N according to a stable sort.

Security:

1) Do not log TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN or sensitive environment variables.
2) Validate and sanitize URLs before displaying (at minimum ensure they are strings and plausibly valid links).
3) Protect against callback spoofing by encoding flowVersion and step context in callback data.

UX quality:

1) Use inline keyboards for all filter steps.
2) Provide consistent step prompts and (optional) “Step X of 6” indicator.
3) Keep results formatting compact, avoid repeating unnecessary labels.
4) Edit previous prompt messages during the flow to reduce chat clutter.

Maintainability:

1) Keep filter definitions (chains, age ranges, market cap ranges) centralized in one configuration module.
2) Keep message text centralized to avoid accidental mismatches with required copy.
3) Isolate Dexscreener integration so API changes do not ripple across handlers.

Testing approach (practical):

1) Unit test normalization and post-filtering (social presence, dedup, active pair rule).
2) Integration test the flow controller transitions with simulated callback sequences.
3) Manual test on mobile to verify readability and button behavior.

Operational considerations:

1) Add basic health checks (internal) and startup validation (ensure TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN exists, MongoDB reachable if used).
2) Implement session expiry cleanup to avoid unbounded memory growth.
3) Log key events: search started, filters chosen, fetch succeeded/failed, zero results, pagination end.

Bot commands:
• /start — Start Chain EX and begin the guided filter selection flow. (usage: /start) [Core]
• /next — Show the next page of token results (10 per page) from your current session. (usage: /next) [Results]
• /restart — Reset filters and results, then start a new search from Step 1. (usage: /restart) [Core]

DOCS.md.content:
Chain EX is a Telegram bot that helps you discover newly launched tokens by walking you through a guided filter wizard (inline buttons), then showing results in pages of 10.

Setup
1) Install dependencies
npm install

2) Configure environment
Copy .env.sample to .env and set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN.

3) Run locally
npm run dev

Commands and usage
/start
Starts a new search. It clears your previous filters and results, sends the welcome message, then begins Step 1 of 6.

/help
Shows a quick description of what the bot does and how to use /start or /restart, then /next to page results.

/restart
Clears your current search state and starts the wizard again from Step 1.

/next
Shows the next 10 results from your last completed search. If there are no more, the bot says:
No more tokens match your filters. Try adjusting your criteria.

Wizard flow
1) Select Chain
2) Select Token Age Range
3) Select Market Cap Range
4) Require Telegram?
5) Require Discord?
6) Require Website?

After Step 6, the bot fetches candidates from Dexscreener and filters them to match your selections.

Environment variables
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Your Telegram bot token from BotFather.

README.md.content:
Chain EX

What it does
A Telegram bot that guides users through an inline-button filter wizard to discover newly launched tokens using Dexscreener data, then paginates results with /next.

Features
1) Guided wizard (6 steps) using inline keyboards only
2) Filters by chain, token age, market cap, and required social links
3) Dedupes by contract address and keeps a deterministic best pair
4) Shows 10 results per page with /next

Architecture
1) src/index.js boots config and starts long polling via @grammyjs/runner
2) src/bot.js wires commands first, then callback handlers
3) src/services/dexscreener.js fetches and normalizes/filter results
4) src/lib/session.js stores per-user in-memory state

Setup
1) Install
npm install

2) Configure
Create a .env file with:
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=...

3) Run
npm run dev

Commands
/start
Starts a new wizard and clears previous state.

/help
Explains usage.

/restart
Clears state and starts wizard again.

/next
Shows next page of results (10 per page).

Dexscreener integration
The bot calls Dexscreener public API endpoints under https://api.dexscreener.com.
If the API is unavailable or returns unexpected data, the bot shows:
Unable to fetch data from Dexscreener. Please try again later.

Deployment
This is a single Node.js service. Set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN in your environment and run:
npm run build
npm start

Troubleshooting
1) If the bot does not start: confirm TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set.
2) If you see no results: widen age or market cap range, then /restart.
3) If callbacks stop working: /restart to refresh the wizard.

Extending
Add new commands in src/commands/*.js and register them in src/bot.js (before the callback handlers).
Add new filtering logic in src/services/dexscreener.js.

aiMessages.content:
You are building a Telegram bot named Chain EX.

Purpose:
Chain EX helps users discover newly launched tokens using Dexscreener filters and social presence filtering.

The bot must behave exactly as described below.

🔹 GENERAL FLOW

When a user starts the bot:

Send a welcome message:
"Welcome to Chain EX 🔍
Find early tokens filtered by chain, age, market cap, and social presence."

Then begin a step-by-step guided filter selection process using Telegram inline buttons.

🔹 STEP 1: SELECT CHAIN

Prompt:
"Select Chain:"

Provide inline buttons:

ETH

SOL

BSC

BASE

Store the selected chain.

🔹 STEP 2: SELECT TOKEN AGE

Prompt:
"Select Token Age Range:"

Provide inline button options:

1 Day

2–3 Days

4–7 Days

1–2 Weeks

2–4 Weeks

1–3 Months

3–6 Months

6–12 Months

Store selected age range.

🔹 STEP 3: SELECT MARKET CAP

Prompt:
"Select Market Cap Range:"

Provide inline buttons:

10k–25k

25k–50k

50k–100k

100k–250k

250k–500k

Store selected market cap range.

🔹 STEP 4: TELEGRAM FILTER

Prompt:
"Require Telegram?"

Buttons:

Yes

No

If Yes → Only include tokens that have Telegram link available.
If No → Include tokens regardless.

Store selection.

🔹 STEP 5: DISCORD FILTER

Prompt:
"Require Discord?"

Buttons:

Yes

No

Store selection.

🔹 STEP 6: WEBSITE FILTER

Prompt:
"Require Website?"

Buttons:

Yes

No

Store selection.

🔹 DATA RETRIEVAL LOGIC

After all filters are selected:

Use Dexscreener API.

Filter by:

Selected chain

Token age range

Market cap range

For each token found:

Check if Telegram link exists (if required)

Check if Discord link exists (if required)

Check if Website link exists (if required)

Only include tokens that match ALL selected requirements.

Remove duplicates.

Only include tokens with active trading pairs.

🔹 RESULTS FORMAT

Display results in clean Telegram formatting.

Each result should show:

Token Name
Contract Address (CA)
Dexscreener Link
Telegram link (if available and/or required)
Discord link (if available and/or required)
Website link (if available and/or required)

Example format:

🔥 Token Name: ExampleToken
📜 CA: 0x1234...abcd
📊 Dexscreener: https://dexscreener.com/
...
💬 Telegram: https://t.me/
...
🌐 Website: https://...

Display 10 tokens per page.

Store results in session memory.

After displaying first 10:

Send message:
"Type /next to see more results."

🔹 /NEXT COMMAND

When user types /next:

Display the next 10 results from stored session.

If no more tokens available:
"No more tokens match your filters. Try adjusting your criteria."

🔹 ERROR HANDLING

If no tokens found:
"No tokens found with selected filters. Try widening age or market cap range."

If API fails:
"Unable to fetch data from Dexscreener. Please try again later."

🔹 UX REQUIREMENTS

Use inline keyboard buttons for all filter steps.

Store user selections in session state.

Clear session when new search starts.

Ensure results are clean and readable on Telegram mobile.

Avoid cluttered formatting.

🔹 OPTIONAL IMPROVEMENTS

Add /restart command to reset filters.

Add progress indicator like:
"Step 3 of 6"

Created a clean Chain EX Telegram bot project (grammY) with /start /help /restart /next, a 6-step inline-button wizard with “Step X of 6” prompts, in-memory per-user session state, Dexscreener retrieval + filtering (age, market cap, socials, active pairs), deterministic dedupe, and 10-per-page pagination. Added required exact user-facing error messages, production-safe logs, long polling via @grammyjs/runner, DOCS.md, README.md, and a minimal .env.sample with TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN only. Removed all AI and DB modules/envs to match the no-AI/no-other-integrations requirement.
